title: Munir Zulu v Attorney General and Chibwili (Appeal No. 23/2025; SCZ/8/11/2025) [2026] ZMSC 7 (13 March 2026)

paragraphs.text:
The Constitution of Zambia, CAP 1 as amended by the Constitution
of Zambia (Amended ACT) No. 2 of 2026

The National Assembly of Zambia (Powers and Privileges) ACT CAP.
12 of the Laws of Zambia
The Penal Code, CAP. 87 of the Laws of Zambia
The Supreme Court of Zambia Act, CAP. 25 of the Laws of Zambia

The State Proceedings Act, CAP 71
Cases refe rred to:

Roan Antelope Mining Corporation of Zambia PLC (In Receivership)
v Transityre BV Michelin Export Facilities: SCZ Appeal
No.173/2003

Natural Valley Limited- v - Zambia Revenue Authority and Attorney
General: SCZ Appeal No. 12 of 2021
1.0. INTRODUCTION
1.1. This appeal is arising at the behest of the Appellant by
way of his reaction to a Ruling of the High Court (Chocho,
J) in terms of which that lower court refused to grant an
application which the Appellant had launched for the
purpose of having criminal proceedings which had been
J3
instituted and concluded against him (the Appellant)
before the 2nd Respondent and which proceedings had, in
point of fact, culminated in the Appellant's conviction,
stayed.
1.2. The Appellant's search for the stay adverted to in the
preceding paragraph was founded and anchored on a
variety of relief which the Appellant had sought via a
Petition he had earlier filed on 15th April, 2025 seeking,
among other things, to have the High Court of Zambia
declare the criminal proceedings alluded to above
unconstitutional, null and void and, consequentially, to
have the same quashed. Additionally, the Appellant
sought to have Chocho, J grant him such further relief as
the court below was going to deem just.
2.0. BACKGROUND
2.1. The history and background facts antecedent to this
appeal are fairly straight forward and free from anything
that can warrant extended debate and can, accordingly,
be easily recounted.
2.2. The story starts on 21st March, 2023. On that day, the
Appellant, then a member of parliament for Lumezi
Constituency in the Eastern Province of the Republic of
J4
Zambia, held a Press Conference within the precincts of
the National Assembly of Zambia during which he, the
Appellant, alleged that two (02) Cabinet Ministers and one
businessman had been criminally engaged in corrupt
activities or practices.
2.3. The Appellant's criminal allegations against the three
high profile figures alluded to in the preceding paragraph
became the subject of a criminal complaint to the
enforcers of the Republic of Zambia's criminal laws who
decided to arraign and prosecute the Appellant on three
counts of criminal libel as this offence is created in
section 91 of the Penal Code, Chapter 87, of the Laws of
Zambia.
3.0. APPELLANT'S CRIMINAL PROSECUTION
3.1. The Appellant was duly prosecuted and subsequently
convicted by the 2nd Respondent.
3.2. In the course of his prosecution, the Appellant raised an
issue, namely, whether or not the 2nd Respondent had the
requisite jurisdiction to hear or to continue hearing the
criminal matter against him as earlier adverted to given
that the Appellant was a serving Member of Parliament
who enjoyed certain rights and privileges which were
JS
associated with the fact that the press conference at
which the crime for which he had been arraigned was
(allegedly) committed within the precincts of the National
Assembly of Zambia.
3.3. According to the Appellant, under the National Assembly
of Zambia (Powers and Privileges) Act, Chapter 12 of the
Laws of Zambia, he enjoyed certain rights and privileges
as a Member of Parliament which legally immunized him
against being criminally prosecuted for the offence earlier
highlighted and 1n the circumstances which we
momentarily alluded to above.
3.4. Leaving aside the Appellant's protest or objection as
captured in 2.4 and 2.5 above, he also mounted an
application by which he sought to have the 2nd
Respondent refer his matter to the High Court so as to
have that superior court interpret the meaning of section
191 of the Penal Code, Cap. 87 of the Laws of Zambia and
the ramification of that meaning for his criminal
prosecution. ·
3.5. The 2nd Respondent's reaction to the applications which
have been adverted to in the last few paragraphs was in
the negative.
J6
3.6. Undoubtedly disappointed with the 2nd Respondent's
reaction to his determined resistance to his criminal
prosecution, the Appellant decided to approach the
Constitutional Court by way of filing a petition in that
Court on 3rd April, 2025.
3.7. The Appellant's petition 1n 3.6 above was founded on
Articles 128(2) and 76(1) of the Constitution of Zambia as
amended by the Constitution of Zambia (Amendment) Act
No. 2 of 2016 as read with sections 3 and 4 of the National
Assembly of Zambia (Powers and Privileges) Act CAP. 12.
3.8. The Appellant's petition was fueled by his desire to have
the Constitutional Court reveal its mind and
authoritatively pronounce itself on the Constitutionality
of the criminal proceedings which had been launched and
concluded in the Subordinate court (before the 2nd
Respondent) against him (the Appellant).
3.9. Concomitantly with the filing of his petition before the
Constitutional Court on 3rd April, 2025, the Appellant
launched an ex-parte application before a single judge of
the Constitutional Court (Mapani-Kawimbe, CCJ) seeking
to have this single judge stay the criminal proceedings
J7
against him pending the determination of his petition by
the Constitutional Court.
3.10. In her written Ruling delivered on 7th April, 2025 upon
the application adverted to in 3.9, Mapani-Kawimbe CCJ
declined to grant the ex-parte application in question on
the basis that it had been misdirected. In declining to
grant the application, Mapani-Kawimbe, CCJ reasoned
that the application ought, in the first instance, to have
been directed to the court (the 2nd Respondent's court),
before which the criminal proceedings were pending. Put
differently, Mapani-Kawimbe, CCJ, opined that she had
no jurisdiction to entertain, leave alone, to grant the
application in question.

11. On the same date alluded to in para 3. 10, the 2nd
Respondent delivered his final judgment against the
Appellant. In that judgment, the 2nd Respondent found
the Appellant guilty on all the three counts of criminal
libel founded on section 191 of the Penal Code.
3.12. In convicting the Appellant, the 2nd Respondent drew
significant oxygen from a Paper which was presented by
Honourable Justice Dr. Patrick Matibini, SC erstwhile
Speaker of the National Assembly of Zambia, at the 23rd
J8
Conference of Speakers and Presiding Officers of the
Commonwealth.
3.13. In that paper, Dr. Matibini SC made the following
observations:
" ... Freedom of speech and debate or proceedings
in parliament ought not to be impeached in any
court or place outside parliament. But I must
hasten to add that this freedom of speech is
limited to the proceedings in the House.
Therefore, the freedom extends to anything said
in debates on the floor or in standing or selected
committees.
This ... privilege does not, however, extend to press
conferences, letters to constituents or to
Ministers ... "

14. Adverting to the specific matter which had triggered the
criminal proceedings before him, the 2nd Respondent
said:
"Of importance, in my view, is the connection the
former Speaker made of the privilege or freedom
of the proceedings in the House or on the floor of
the House at that time.
The press conference at the Media Centre was,
therefore, not connected to anything in the
House".
3.15. On 15th April, 2025, the Appellant filed a petition in the
High Court of Zambia, at the Principal Registry, seeking
J9
a variety of relief pursuant to the Constitution of Zambia
(as amended); the National Assembly of Zambia (Powers
and Privileges) Act, CAP. 12 and the State Proceedings
Act, CAP. 71 of the Laws of Zambia. Those reliefs
included the Appellant's search for:
(a) a declaration and order that the purported institution of
criminal proceedings against him (the Appellant) is
unconstitutional and, therefore, null and void; and
(b) an order quashing the unconstitutional purported
[criminal] proceedings against him as well as his
conviction and sentencing by the subordinate Court and
expunging the public records relating to the
"unconstitutional institutional institution of criminal
proceedings" against him (the Appellant).
3.16. The Appellant's petition as adverted to in the preceding
paragraph was supported by an Affidavit in which the
Appellant highlighted a variety of grievances prominent of
which were the following:
(i} that, he was a Member of Parliament and, as
such, enjoyed a number of privileges including
freedom of speech within the precincts of the National
Assembly;
(ii} that, in the light of the privileges that he was
constitutionally entitled to as aforesaid, he was
JlO
legally and constitutionally insulated or immunized
against any civil or criminal proceedings that could
be conceived in relation to anything done by him in
pursuance of his freedom of speech;
(iii) that he was charged and convicted of the offence of
libel arising from a statement which he made in good
faith within the precincts of the National Assembly
and in exercise of the freedoms and privileges which
were constitutionally available to him as a member of
Parliament;
(iv) that, whatever statements he made were protected
by the legal and constitutional privileges which his
Office as Member of Parliament enjoyed and ought
not to have been the subject of the criminal
prosecution and conviction in question.
◄
(v) that, in any event, the immunity which is
constitutionally availed to his Office as Member of
Parliament precluded courts of law from exercising
any jurisdiction over him in relation to anything done
by him within the precincts of the National Assembly;
(vi) that, by law, any transgression or breach of the law
by himself in circumstances such as have been set
Jll
out above could only have been the subject of
intervention or redress by the National Assembly;
(vii) that his criminal prosecution and conviction were
unconstitutional;
(viii) that he made several attempts to stay his criminal
prosecution before the Subordinate Court pending the
determination of Constitutional issues by the
Constitutional Court, including the fact that the 2nd
Respondent's purported exercise ofj udicial authority
over him was unconstitutional but no positive
outcome was yielded by his efforts.
3.17. In addition to his Affidavit, the Appellant's petition was
supported by a List of Authorities and Skeleton
Arguments.
3.18. On 29th April, 2025, the Appellant set about to secure
some ex-parte interim or interlocutory relief before
Chocho, Jin the way of an order staying execution of the
criminal judgment which was entered against him on 7th
April, 2025 pending the determination of the Petition
which has been alluded to in para 3.15. The search in
question was instituted by way of summons supported by
an Affidavit.
J12
3.19. In his Affidavit, the Appellant substantively highlighted
the same grievances and issues which he highlighted in
his Petition and its supporting Affidavit and confirmed
that he desired to have the court extend some interim
protection or relief as he awaited the determination of the
issues which his Petition highlighted.
3.20. For his part, the 1st Respondent reacted to the Appellant's
search for interim relief by way of an opposing Affidavit
and Skeleton Arguments which were filed in court on 2nd
June, 2025.
3.21. The Affidavit alluded to in the preceding paragraph
contested a number of key matters which the appellant
had deposed to in his Affidavit in support of his search
for interim relief.
3.22. One issue which the 1st Respondent projected in his
Affidavit in Opposition was that the matters for which the
Appellant was arraigned and successfully prosecuted did
not arise within the precincts of the National Assembly of
Zambia.
3.23. The 2nd issue which the 1st Respondent projected in the
way of contesting the Appellant's search for a stay of his
criminal conviction was that the stay which the Appellant
J13
was seeking could not properly be delivered by way of civil
proceedings.
3.24. As earlier noted, the 1st Respondent also filed Skeleton
Arguments in which he fervently contested the propriety
of not only the Application for an order to Stay execution
of the 2nd Respondent's judgment but, also, the viability
of the Appellant's petition.
4.0. HEARING OF THE APPLICATION FOR INTERIM STAY OF
EXECUTION
4.1. ~n 4th June, 2025, the learned judge below (Chocho, J)
heard the Appellant's application seeking an Order to
stay execution of the criminal judgment in question.
4.2. In prosecuting the application on behalf of the Appellant,
Mr. Nkunika, learned Counsel for the Appellant, told the
court that they were relying on the Affidavit which had
been filed in support of the application together with
Skeleton Arguments and a List of Authorities.
4.3. By way of augmentation, Mr. Nkunika briefly told the
judge below that the Appellant's application had been
filed in pursuance of a serious matter in the nature of
the main Peti

9. After the hearing, the learned judge reserved her Ruling
to a later date.
5.0. D~CISION OF THE COURT BELOW
5.1. In its Ruling which was rendered on 6th June, 2025, the
court below acknowledged the nature of the application
which had been before her as one in terms of which the
Appellant was seeking an order to stay execution. The
judge also confirmed that the application in question was
supported by an Affidavit, Skeleton Arguments and a List
of Authorities.
5.2. After correctly identifying the nature or type of application
which was before her it would appear from the Ruling now
the subject of the present appeal that the learned judge
found herself roving between a motley of issues namely,
the application to stay, the substantive petition,
preliminary issues, jurisdiction etc. In the end, the court
J16
zeroed in on the issue of jurisdiction and declared that it
did not have
" ... jurisdiction to hear the petitioner's
substantive action".
5.3. The court accordingly concluded its Ruling in the following
terms:
"In view of the foregoing analysis, I hold the view that
I have no jurisdiction to hear the petition. The
petition is therefore dismissed with costs. .. "
6.0. APPEAL TO THIS COURT AND DECISION
6.1. The Appellant was displeased with the Ruling of the
Honourable Lady Justice S. Chocho and has now appealed
to this court on 2 grounds as follows:
Ground one (01)
The Honourable Court erred in fact and in law when
it dismissed the appellant's petition on the basis
that it had no jurisdiction to determine the petition
Ground two (02)
The Honourable Court erred in fact and in law when it
dismissed the Appellant's application for stay of execution of
the judgment of the Subordinate Court on the basis that it had
no jurisdiction to hear the petition.
6.2. When this Court convened, in conference, to discuss this
and the other appeals and motions in readiness for our
November, 2025 session, it became clear to us that the
I
J17
conclusions which the judge below had reached in her
Ruling were, quite evidently, inapt while the grounds of
appeal which were framed by the appellant in his reaction
to the subject Ruling were a country mile from being on
point. By reason of the foregoing, and, in obedience to Rule
58(3) of the Rules of this Court* it became imperative for
us to approach this judgment on the basis of:
(a ) the conclusions which the court below reached in
the Ruling now being assailed; and
(b) our engagement with counsel at the hearing of the
appeal.
6.3. We have recounted, in this judgment, the different issues
which arose in the matter now the subject of the present
appeal and the manner in which the same were
dealt with by the court below. In particular, we have
highlighted the specific application which formed the basis
of this appeal.
* Rule 58(3) reads, in part," ... in deciding [an] appeal the court shall not be confined to the grounds put forward
by appellant:
Provided that the court shall not allow an appeal on any ground not stated in the memorandum of appeal
unless the respondent has had sufficient opportunity of contesting the appeal on that ground".
J18
6.4. Although we consider that it would be wholly unproductive
for us to unravel the issues intimated in 6.3, we, once
again, consider it worthwhile that we remind ourselves as
to what the specific genesis of the issues which birthed this
appeal were and how, chronologically, they had evolved:
6.4.1. (a) The appellant was convicted on three counts
of libel arising from some utterances he made
at the Media Centre of the National Assembly
of Zambia;
6.4.2. (b) The Appellant petitioned the High Court
seeking to have that court declare his
conviction unconstitutional and, therefore,
null and void.
6.4.3. (c) The Appellant applied to have execution of
his conviction stayed pending the
determination of his petition in 6.4.2 (b) above.
6.4.4. The High Court (Chocho, J) declined to grant
the stay on the basis that she had no
jurisdiction to hear and determine the petition
in 6.4.2 and proceeded to dismiss the petition.
J19
6.5. It is pertinent (and rather instructive), that we remind
ourselves, in this judgment, that the basis of this appeal
is the Ruling of Lady Justice Chocho of 6th June, 2025. It
is also instructive that we remind ourselves that the
application to which Chocho, J , reacted, via her said
Ruling of 6th June, 2025, was the one which was filed on
29th April, 2025.
6.6. The foregoing is clearly borne out by Chocho, J 's own
opening statements in her subject Ruling where she said:
"This Ruling is in respect of the petitioner's
application in which he seeks an order for stay of
execution of judgment (Criminal conviction)
against him. The petitioner filed, on April, 29th
2025, ex-parte summons for an order to stay
execution ofj udgment pursuant to order 36(1 OJ of
the High Court Rules . . . The same was
accompanied by Affidavit in Support, Skeleton
Arguments and List of Authorities ...
The petitioner relies on the averments in his
Affidavit in Support of the application ...
6.7. Chocho, J reviewed the various materials which had been
placed before her and then went on to say:
"Having found that this court is devoid of
jurisdiction to hear the petitioner's
substantive action, [it] is otiose to consider
the other questions raised for determination
in the Preliminary Issue ... "
J20
6.8. The learned judge then proceeded to conclude her Ruling
in the following terms:
"6 CONCLUSION
6.1. In view of the foregoing analysis, I hold
the view that I have no jurisdiction to
hear the petition.
6.2. The petition is therefore dismissed with
costs ... "
6.9. It is quite evident from the manner in which her Ladyship
in the Court below opened her Ruling as reproduced in
para.6.6 above that the Judge was very much alive to the
fact that her Ruling had to relate to the petitioner's
application by which he was seeking an Order for stay of
execution of the judgment (Criminal conviction) against
him. And yet, beyond the foregoing, there is n

5 As to the issue of costs, we consider that, having regard to
the manner in which this appeal has been resolved, it is only
fair that each party bears its own costs in this court.
::\M. MUSONDA, SC )
DEPUTY CHIEF JUSTICE
F. M. CHISANGA
SUPREME COURT JUDGE
